front: Requirements are unstable, customer value is best proven incrementally, and the team can deliver working product every few weeks. Which development approach should you recommend, and why?
back: Adaptive/agile. High uncertainty and frequent change favor short feedback loops, backlog prioritization, and incremental value delivery over a fixed predictive baseline.
tip: 2026 ECO Process Task 1 explicitly tests recommending predictive, adaptive/agile, or hybrid.

front: Scope is well defined, change is rare, and schedule/cost baselines must be locked early for a fixed-price contract. Which development approach fits best?
back: Predictive (plan-driven). Stable requirements and contractual need for fixed baselines support detailed upfront planning, sequential execution, and formal change control.
tip: Contract type and baseline rigidity are strong predictive cues on the exam.

front: Hardware/infrastructure must be engineered with fixed design gates, while software features still evolve with user feedback. Which approach should you recommend?
back: Hybrid. Use predictive (or stage-gate) control where uncertainty is low and compliance/design locks matter; use adaptive delivery where requirements remain uncertain and feedback adds value.
tip: Different workstreams can legitimately use different approaches on one project.

front: What three high-level cues most strongly drive choosing predictive vs adaptive vs hybrid?
back: Requirement/uncertainty level, expected rate of change, and preferred delivery cadence (single big release vs frequent usable increments with feedback).
tip: If two of three cues point one way, that approach usually wins.

front: On the 2026 PMP exam, roughly what share of items is predictive vs adaptive/hybrid across the value-delivery spectrum?
back: About 40% predictive; about 60% adaptive/agile and hybrid combined. Approaches are integrated across People, Process, and Business Environment—not confined to one domain.
tip: Do not assume agile is only a Process-domain topic.

front: A regulated build needs heavy documentation, formal stage approvals, and low tolerance for midstream scope change. Adaptive delivery is popular with the team. What should you recommend?
back: Lean predictive (or hybrid with predictive governance). Regulatory gates, documentation, and low change tolerance outweigh preference for pure agile; adaptive practices can still sit inside controlled phases if they do not bypass compliance.
tip: Team preference never overrides compliance and governance constraints.

front: Product vision is clear, but solution design is highly uncertain and stakeholders can only validate value when they use partial deliverables. Which approach, and what cadence cue matters most?
back: Adaptive/agile with short delivery cadence. Frequent usable increments enable learning and reprioritization; long single-release cadences hide discovery risk.
tip: Cadence is a selection input, not just a scheduling detail.

front: When is hybrid the better recommendation than pure predictive or pure adaptive?
back: When project parts differ: some work has stable requirements/constraints (contracts, compliance, physical construction) while other work has high change or discovery needs (software, UX, innovation). Combine approaches by work package or phase.
tip: Exam trap: hybrid is not 'weaker agile'—it is intentional mix by context.

front: How should development-approach choice shape Scope and Schedule domain practices (PMBOK 8 function domains)?
back: Predictive: define and baseline full scope early; network/critical-path schedule with change control. Adaptive: progressive elaboration via backlog; timeboxed iterations/releases and velocity/throughput tracking instead of a fixed full-scope baseline.
tip: Same domains, different control mechanisms by approach.

front: Stakeholders demand a firm end date and budget, yet requirements still shift weekly. Pure predictive planning keeps failing. What approach and why?
back: Hybrid: hold fixed outer constraints (date/budget/governance) while using adaptive scope prioritization inside the envelope—deliver highest value first and descope lower-value work if needed.
tip: Fixed constraints + unstable scope ≈ hybrid with value-based prioritization.

front: What organizational or team conditions favor adaptive delivery even when some requirements look stable?
back: Available product owner/customer for frequent feedback, cross-functional team that can ship increments, and a culture that accepts changing priorities. Without those enablers, 'agile theater' underperforms.
tip: Approach must match stakeholder engagement and team capability, not only scope uncertainty.

front: A sponsor insists on pure waterfall for a novel digital product with unknown user behavior. What is the PM's best exam-aligned response?
back: Assess uncertainty and recommend adaptive or hybrid with evidence: unknown user behavior needs short feedback loops. Educate on risk of late discovery under pure predictive; propose a tailored approach that still meets governance needs.
tip: Recommend based on project characteristics, not hierarchy alone—then gain buy-in.

front: Per 2026 ECO Process Task 1, what does 'recommend a project management development approach' include beyond naming predictive/adaptive/hybrid?
back: Assess needs, complexity, and magnitude; recommend an execution strategy; create and maintain an integrated plan suited to that approach; and use data to keep decisions aligned with value and constraints.
tip: Selection is the start; the plan and execution strategy must match the choice.

front: Mid-project, uncertainty drops after a pilot and remaining work is well specified under a firm regulatory deadline. Should you stick with pure adaptive?
back: Not necessarily. Re-tailor: shift remaining work toward more predictive planning and control to protect the deadline and compliance path, while keeping adaptive practices only where residual uncertainty remains.
tip: Approach selection is not one-and-done—reassess when context changes.
